section.para: The Statement of Work is the dynamic component of the agreement, containing the specific, negotiable terms of a single project. The SOW addresses the remaining two pillars of a valid contract:

listitem.para: Legal Object: The SOW provides a detailed description of the specific artwork to be created—the "legal object" of the contract.1 This includes scope, deliverables, medium, and style.10

Consideration: The SOW defines the total value exchanged between the parties.1 This is a holistic concept where cash payment is only one component. For the artist, consideration includes their "sweat equity"—the value of their labor, skill, and time—as well as the intellectual property (IP) and the final tangible artwork itself.1 For the organization, consideration is the payment of fees, but can also include other valuable exchanges, such as the granting of a license or a share of future profits.10

section.para: Crucially, the specific nature of the SOW can trigger higher legal standards. For example, a project involving age-restricted content would elevate the Capacity requirement, while a commission involving complex financial instruments would elevate the Consideration standards, demanding more detailed and informed consent from both parties.
